title: RandSet: Randomized Corpus Reduction for Fuzzing Seed Scheduling
data:
Y Xie, K Zhang, Y Liu, R Yang, P Chen, S Wang, D She- arXiv preprint arXiv, 2026
Seed explosion is a fundamental problem in fuzzing seed scheduling, where a fuzzer 
maintains a huge corpus and fails to choose promising seeds. Existing works focus 
on seed prioritization but still suffer from seed explosion since corpus size remains 
huge. We tackle this from a new perspective: corpus reduction, ie, computing a seed 
corpus subset. However, corpus reduction could lead to poor seed diversity and 
large runtime overhead. Prior techniques like cull_queue, AFL-Cmin, and MinSet
Cites: Directed greybox fuzzing

title: Hardfuzz: DataFlow-Guided On-Device Fuzzing for Microcontrollers (Registered Report)
data:
K Feng, J Singer, AK Marnerides- work
Fuzzing firmware on microcontrollers (MCUs) is hard to scale. Rehosting is an ideal 
way to achieve this, but it often loses fidelity and can be slow, while on-device tracing 
support is limited. Standard coverage-guided fuzzing relies on software 
instrumentation, which is costly for MCUs and gives only control-flow signals that 
arrive late for complex checks. We present Hardfuzz, an on-device fuzzer that uses 
definitionuse (def-use) chains to guide exploration. Hardfuzz performs offline static
Cites: AFLNet: a greybox fuzzer for network protocols

data:
Y Tang, M Wu, Y Li, H Liao, J Guo, B Huang- 2026 IEEE/ACM International, 2026
Modern processors are breaking a fundamental rule: backward compatibility within 
their own ISA families. We term this Generational ISA Fragmentation (GIF), where 
newer processors cannot execute instructions supported by prior generations within 
the same ISA family. This phenomenon is exemplified by Intel's removal of AVX-512 
from Alder Lake processors after years of deployment, ARM's inconsistent support for 
SVE across cores, and RISC-V's incompatible vector specifications. GIF causes

data:

Unpredictable costs are a growing concern in serverless computing, where 
applications rely on cloud APIs with complex tiered pricing models. In many 
deployments, API calls dominate expenses, and a single overlooked design choice 
can escalate costs by thousands of dollars. Existing tools fall short: provider 
calculators need unrealistic manual estimates, and dynamic profilers only work post-
deployment. We present Skyler, a static analysis framework for predeployment cost
Cites: Chronos: A timing analyzer for embedded software

title: Analyzing the worst-case behavior of multi-level caches in concurrent real-time systems
data:
T Fischer - 2025

systems featuring multi-level cache hierarchies. First, an analysis of inter-core cache
